title: Memory
summary: SRAM, DRAM, ROM, and register files — storing bits at scale.

sections.heading: Memory Hierarchy
sections.body: Digital systems layer memory by speed, cost, and capacity: registers (fastest, smallest) → SRAM caches → DRAM main memory → flash/disk. On-chip SRAM and register files are designed by digital/VLSI engineers; off-chip DRAM uses specialized processes.
bullets: Register file: small, multi-port, inside CPU/datapath

sections.heading: SRAM Fundamentals
sections.body: Static RAM uses a bistable latch (typically 6 transistors) per bit. Read: assert word line, bit lines develop differential voltage, sense amp amplifies. Write: drive bit lines, assert word line to overwrite cell.
bullets: Column mux selects words or sub-blocks

sections.heading: DRAM and Refresh
sections.body: DRAM stores charge on a capacitor accessed by one transistor. Cells leak — periodic refresh rewrites every row. Higher density than SRAM but slower and needs memory controller.

callout.text: FPGA block RAM (BRAM) is SRAM-based — synchronous, configurable width/depth.

sections.heading: Memory in RTL
sections.body: Verilog models memory as reg arrays: reg [7:0] mem [0:255]. Synthesis maps to BRAM or distributed RAM depending on size and ports. Read-before-write vs write-first behavior must match hardware.
bullets: data_out = mem[addr]; // asynchronous or registered read

sections.heading: FIFOs and Queues
sections.body: FIFO (first-in-first-out) buffers data between clock domains or rate-mismatched blocks. Dual-clock FIFOs need gray-coded pointers and synchronizers. Synchronous FIFOs use single clock with read/write pointers.

quiz.question: SRAM cell typically uses:
quiz.explanation: Classic 6T SRAM cell is a cross-coupled inverter pair with access transistors.

quiz.question: DRAM requires periodic:
quiz.explanation: Capacitor leakage mandates refresh cycles to retain data.

quiz.question: FPGA Block RAM is usually:
quiz.explanation: FPGA BRAM is on-chip SRAM, synchronous and configurable.

quiz.question: Dual-port RAM allows:
options: Two simultaneous accesses to different addresses
quiz.explanation: True dual-port: independent read/write ports in same cycle.

quiz.question: Gray-coded pointers in async FIFO prevent:
quiz.explanation: Gray code ensures synchronizers see at most one bit change.

quiz.question: Register file differs from large SRAM by:
options: Multiple ports optimized for CPU register access
quiz.explanation: Register files are small, highly ported memories in the datapath.

flashcards.back: DRAM denser; SRAM faster, no refresh
Per-byte write mask for sub-word stores
